content.problemStatement:
You have an array AAA of length NNN. In one operation, you can do the following:


Select any subarray AL…RA_{L \ldots R}AL…R​ (1≤L≤R≤N)(1 \le L \le R \le N)(1≤L≤R≤N) and then add iii to the iii-th element of this subarray. Formally, we set Aj:=Aj+(j−L+1)A_j := A_j + (j - L + 1)Aj​:=Aj​+(j−L+1) for all L≤j≤RL \le j \le RL≤j≤R.
For example: if A=[2,1,5,2]A = [2, 1, 5, 2]A=[2,1,5,2] and we apply operation on A2…4A_{2 \ldots 4}A2…4​ then AAA becomes [2,1+1,5+2,2+3]=[2,2,7,5][2, 1 + 1, 5 + 2, 2 + 3] = [2, 2, 7, 5][2,1+1,5+2,2+3]=[2,2,7,5].

The minimum number of such operations required to sort AAA in non-decreasing order is termed as the goodness of AAA.

We want to process QQQ updates on AAA of the following form:


i x\texttt{i x}i x : Set Ai:=xA_i := xAi​:=x where (1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N).
Determine the goodness of AAA after each update.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and QQQ — the size of the array AAA and the number of updates.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.
The next QQQ lines each contain two integers iii and xxx denoting the parameters of the update.


Output Format
For each test case, output QQQ integers, the goodness of the array after each update.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and Q.
- The second line contains N integers A1 … AN.
- Each of the next Q lines contains two integers i and x, meaning set Ai := x.
All indices are 1‑based.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output Q lines. The k‑th line of a test case is the goodness of the array after applying the k‑th update.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1:
Initial array [3,2,1] has drops 3-2=1 and 2-1=1, sum=2 (goodness before any query, not printed).
After first update (3,10) array becomes [3,2,10]; drops: 3-2=1, 2-10=0 → goodness=1.
After second update (2,1) array becomes [3,1,10]; drops: 3-1=2, 1-10=0 → goodness=2.

Test case 2:
Initial [2,2,2,2] already sorted → goodness=0.
First update does not change the array, still 0.
Second update changes last element to 1 → array [2,2,2,1]; only drop is 2-1=1 → goodness=1.

solution.approach:
For an array A the minimum number of allowed operations needed to make it non‑decreasing equals the total amount by which the array "drops" when moving from left to right:
    goodness = Σ_{i=1}^{N-1} max(0, A_i - A_{i+1}).
Proof sketch: Consider any adjacent pair (i,i+1). If A_i ≤ A_{i+1} the pair already satisfies the order and never forces an operation. If A_i > A_{i+1} we must increase A_{i+1} (or later elements) enough to eliminate the drop. The only way to increase A_{i+1} without also increasing A_i is to use an operation whose right end is i (i.e. the operation ends at position i). Such an operation adds exactly 1 to A_i and 0 to A_{i+1}, reducing the drop by 1. Hence each unit of drop requires a distinct operation, and drops are independent, giving the sum formula.

When a single element Ai is changed, only the two adjacent drops that involve Ai can change: the pair (i‑1,i) and the pair (i,i+1). We keep the current answer and update it in O(1) by recomputing those two contributions.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Read the array, compute initial answer = Σ max(0, A[i] - A[i+1]).
2. For each query (i,x):
   a. For neighbor i‑1 (if i>1) compute old = max(0, A[i-1] - A[i]), new = max(0, A[i-1] - x) and add (new‑old) to answer.
   b. For neighbor i (if i<N) compute old = max(0, A[i] - A[i+1]), new = max(0, x - A[i+1]) and add (new‑old) to answer.
   c. Set A[i] = x.
   d. Output the current answer.
All operations are O(1), so total complexity is O(N + Q) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + Q) per test case, i.e. linear in the size of the input.

code.Python3:
import sys

def pos_drop(a, b):
    return a - b if a > b else 0

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it)); q = int(next(it))
        A = [0] + [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]  # 1‑based
        ans = sum(pos_drop(A[i], A[i+1]) for i in range(1, n))
        for _ in range(q):
            idx = int(next(it)); x = int(next(it))
            if idx > 1:
                old = pos_drop(A[idx-1], A[idx])
                new = pos_drop(A[idx-1], x)
                ans += new - old
            if idx < n:
                old = pos_drop(A[idx], A[idx+1])
                new = pos_drop(x, A[idx+1])
                ans += new - old
            A[idx] = x
            out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
